body:
Dear David,

I hope this finds you well and that Emily's clinicals are going smoothly — tell her I've been praying she gets a good preceptor this rotation, not one of those crabby ones who acts like teaching is beneath her.

I'm writing about that walk-tracking gadget you all set me up with a few weeks back. Dot Henderson and I have kept up our morning route rain or shine — down past the community garden and back, same as always — but the app on my phone has stopped logging Tuesday and Thursday altogether. Wednesday and Friday show up fine, plain as day, but it's like Tuesday and Thursday never happened. Dot thinks I'm losing my marbles, I told her it's the machine that's confused, not me.

Could someone look into whether it's a syncing hiccup? I'd like an accurate record since Karen likes to check in on it now and then. No rush — I've got my PBS dramas to keep me company this evening regardless.

Warmly,
Joanne Lawton

Dear Joanne,

Thank you for the kind words about Emily — she mentioned the same thing about wanting a good preceptor, so I'll let her know you're pulling for her. That made me smile.

I took a look at your account, and it does appear there was a sync gap on the two days you mentioned. It looks like the app failed to connect to the server overnight those Tuesdays and Thursdays, so the walks recorded on your phone never made it up to save. The walks themselves weren't lost, just stuck locally — I've gone ahead and pulled that data back in, so your log with Dot should now show the full week correctly.

To keep this from happening again, could you check that the app has permission to run in the background and isn't getting closed out overnight? I can walk you through the settings next time we talk if that's easier than doing it solo.

Glad to hear you and Dot are keeping up the routine no matter what — that consistency really does count for something. Enjoy your dramas tonight.

Best,
David Mitchell

Good morning, David — Joanne Lawton here, hope this finds you well over there. I'll get right to it: that little glucose tracker app you all set me up with has been acting contrary all week. Every morning after Dot Henderson and I finish our walk, I do my fasting check and try to log it before I take my morning medications, and Tuesday through today the app just spins and never saves the entry. I've been a nurse forty-some years and I don't scare easy over a machine, but I don't like flying blind either. Can you tell me if there's a fix, or do I need a new gadget? I'd rather know now than find out it's been failing me a month from now.

Good morning, Mrs. Lawton, thank you for the detailed note — it makes troubleshooting much easier. A sync failure right after a fasting entry usually points to a Bluetooth pairing issue between the reader and the app rather than anything wrong with your readings themselves; nothing you're recording is being lost, it's just not uploading. Could you confirm the model number on the back of your reader and whether you've updated the app in the last week? In the meantime, I'd suggest jotting the numbers in the little paper log that came with the kit as a backup each morning, right alongside when you take your medications, so there's no gap either way. I'll also flag your account so if this keeps up past Friday we send a replacement reader at no charge.

David, bless you for the quick reply — I found the model number, it's the DX-220, and yes I let the app update last night before PBS came on. This morning's check went through just fine, so maybe that update was all it needed. I did start keeping the paper log like you said, old habits die hard anyway, I used to chart everything by hand for thirty years at the hospital. If it acts up again before Friday I'll write you, otherwise let's call this settled. Tell whoever writes your software that an old nurse says well done. Take care, and thank you for not making me feel foolish for asking.

Dear David,

I hope Emily's clinical rotations are treating her well this week – tell her I'm proud of her. I wanted to write and thank you for the DVD box set you and Karen sent over. I've been working through the old Poirot episodes on PBS in the evenings, and it's become quite the little ritual. Dot Henderson even asked if she could come by Thursdays to watch with me before our morning walk the next day.

I'm still keeping to my nine-thirty bedtime, just like Karen fusses at me to do. There's something about a good mystery and a cup of chamomile that settles a person down properly, better than any pill, if you ask me. My grandmother used to say the mind needs a slow wind-down same as a clock, and I believe she was right.

Anyway, just wanted to check in and say the routine is going well. Give my love to Tyler and Hailey.

Warmly,
Joanne

Hi Joanne,

That's wonderful to hear, and yes, Emily's clinicals are going great – she called last night full of stories, and I passed along that you're proud of her. She lit up hearing it.

I'm glad the PBS routine and the nine-thirty bedtime are sticking. Karen mentioned Dot joining in on Thursdays too, sounds like a nice little tradition forming. Out of curiosity, are you finding it easy to wind down after, or does the mystery-solving keep your mind racing a bit before sleep? Just want to make sure the routine's actually helping rather than working against you.

Let me know if there's anything else you need – happy to help track down more shows if you run through this set too quickly.

Best,
David

David,

No, it settles me down just fine, thank you for asking. I've found the trick is to let the last episode be a lighter one – nothing too twisty right before bed, or my mind does keep chewing on it like a dog with a bone. Dot and I have taken to doing the heavier mysteries earlier in the evening and saving the gentler ones, the Miss Marples and such, for right before I turn in.

Honestly, between the walk in the morning and the shows at night, my days have a nice shape to them again. It's a small thing, but at my age you learn small things matter more than people think.

I did want to ask, though – do you know if there's a way to set the television to turn itself off after a show ends? I have a habit of nodding off in the chair and waking up at midnight to some infomercial blaring at me.

Take care,
Joanne

Dear Joanne,

That made me laugh – I can picture it exactly. Yes, most PBS-connected smart TVs and streaming boxes have a sleep timer built in, usually under the display or general settings menu. If you tell me what TV or remote you have, I can walk you through setting it for say 45 minutes so it clicks off right after your show ends, no more infomercials at midnight.

I'm really glad the mornings with Dot and the evenings with the dramas are giving your days some shape again – that kind of steady rhythm makes a real difference, especially heading into the busier summer months.

Let me know the TV model whenever you get a chance, and I'll send simple step-by-step instructions. Give Dot our best, and I'll keep you posted on Emily's clinical schedule too.

Warmly,
David

Subject: Re: Grocery list mix-up

David, honey, I need your help with something silly. I went to text Karen the grocery list this morning like I always do before my walk with Dot Henderson, and my phone froze right up on me — sent the same message about four times and now it won't let me type anything new into that thread at all. I don't want her showing up at Fred Meyer four times over for the same eggs and oat flour.

I know you're not the phone people, but you're the only one who ever gets these gadgets to behave for me, and Brian's no help with this sort of thing. Can you take a look, or tell me who I ought to call?

Thank you, dear. Tell Emily I'm praying her clinicals go smooth this week.

Fondly,
Joanne

Subject: Re: Grocery list mix-up

Hi Joanne,

That sounds like the messaging app got stuck trying to resend — happens more than you'd think. I went ahead and cleared out the stuck messages on your phone remotely, so the thread with Karen should be back to normal now. If you want to double check, just open it up and try sending a quick "testing" message before you send the real list.

Also, for next time, if it ever freezes again, holding down the message and hitting "try again" usually clears it faster than waiting it out. I'll check back with you in a few days to make sure it's still behaving.

Glad to help, and I'll pass along your message to Emily — she'll appreciate it. Enjoy your walk with Dot!

Best,
David

Subject: Re: Gmail API — still acting up

David, hello again —

I hate to be the squeaky wheel, but that Gmail business we talked about last week hasn't quite sorted itself out. I go to check my mail after my walk with Dot Henderson of a morning, and the messages are just sitting there refusing to sync like they did before — Karen says it's the "connection" but you know that's Greek to me. I finally get my grocery list texted off to her and then I sit down with my coffee to look at email and half of it's from three days ago.

I don't want to make a fuss, and I know these things take time, same as anything worth doing right — I used to tell the young nurses that too, patience is its own medicine. But I did want to follow up since it's been a few days and nothing's changed on my end. Is there something else you all need from me, or a setting Brian could look at for me? He's not much for computers either, bless him, but he tries.

Hoping to hear something before my programs come on tonight.

Warmly,
Joanne Lawton

Subject: Re: Gmail API — still acting up

Dear Mrs. Lawton,

Thank you for following up, and I'm sorry the sync issue is still giving you trouble — I know how frustrating it is to open your mail and find it's out of date. I looked back at the notes from your last message and I want to make sure I have this right before we go further.

Could you tell me roughly what time of day the mail usually stops updating, and whether it happens on the desktop, a phone, or both? Also, if you could check whether you're still signed in (sometimes the app quietly logs a person out after a password change or an update), that would help us narrow it down quite a bit. No rush on my end, but the sooner I have those details the sooner I can get this resolved for you properly.

If it's easier, Karen or Emily are welcome to relay the answers on your behalf — I know Emily's been busy with her nursing clinicals, but even a quick note back from you works just fine too.

Best,
David

Subject: Re: Gmail API — still acting up

David —

Well, good news for once. I had Emily look at it Sunday between her clinical shifts, bless her, she's sharper with these things than her old grandmother. Turns out I had indeed gotten signed out somewhere back around when the app updated itself — didn't even know a person could get logged out without doing anything, seems backwards to me, like getting locked out of your own kitchen. She signed me back in and had me watch her do it so I could manage next time.

It's been syncing along fine since, mail showing up right when it should, even caught this morning's before my walk with Dot. So I think whatever you all fixed on your end plus that sign-in did the trick together.

I do appreciate you sticking with me on this one, David — you've always been patient with your mother-in-law's fussing over these gadgets, and I don't take that for granted. Tell Karen I got the grocery list sent off already too, so no worries there.

Take care of yourself,
Joanne

Dear David,

I'm writing again about that calendar business we spoke on last week. I thought it had settled down after you had me sign back into the Google account, but this morning my whole week looked like a jumbled deck of cards. My walk with Dot Henderson showed up three times on Tuesday, and Wednesday was empty as a church on Monday, when I know for a fact I've got Emily's nursing clinical schedule sitting right there and my herbalist appointment with a new client at eleven.

I don't want to sound like an old woman who can't work a computer, because I spent thirty-some years charting on paper and then on machines that beeped at you, so I'm not entirely helpless here. But whatever this API thing is doing, it isn't behaving. I need this calendar right so I don't miss texting Karen the grocery list before she does her shopping run, and so I don't stand Dot up on a Tuesday morning.

Can you tell me plainly what's going on and what you need from me to fix it?

Warmly,
Joanne Lawton
Following up on google-calendar-api — follow-up.
Following up on google-calendar-api — follow-up.
Following up on google-calendar-api — follow-up.

Dear David,

Following up on the trouble I mentioned last week with that Google Drive business — the shared folder where I keep my herbal remedy notes and the recipe cards I've been typing up for the church ladies still won't sync properly on this tablet. Half my files show up, half don't, and I nearly lost the tincture dosage chart I'd been working on for two days.

I know you said something about an API key or permissions needing to be refreshed on your end. I don't pretend to understand the mechanics of it, but I trust you'll sort it out same as you always do. Karen tried to help me over the phone but she's not much better at this than I am, bless her.

No rush — I'm not touching that folder again until you tell me it's safe. Dot and I have our walk in the morning, so I'll be up early anyway if you need to call.

Warmly,
Joanne

Hi Joanne,

Thanks for the details — that helps a lot. I dug into it and it looks like the Drive API authorization token tied to your account had expired, which is why some files were syncing and others just silently failed. I've refreshed the connection and reset the folder permissions on the shared "Herbal Notes" directory so it should pull everything through cleanly now.

Could you open the folder on the tablet once more and confirm your tincture dosage chart is back and showing the latest edits? Sometimes it takes a few minutes to fully reconcile, so give it a little time before worrying.

Also, going forward, I set it to auto-refresh every 30 days so this shouldn't sneak up on you again. Tell Emily good luck with her clinicals this week, and I'll check in with you and Karen at dinner Sunday to make sure it's holding steady.

Take care,
David

Dear David,

I'm writing to follow up on the ticket I opened last week about the WhatsApp API messages for my little herbal remedy circle. Since the update, half my Tuesday reminders about the elderberry tincture class go out fine, but the other half just sit there with one gray checkmark and never seem to land on Dot Henderson's phone, and she's the one who organizes half our walking group besides. I know it's not glamorous work, but forty years of charting vitals taught me to be precise about what's actually happening versus what folks assume is happening, so I wanted to give you the real picture.

I've already restarted the app twice and made sure my number is still verified. Could you check on your end whether something changed with the API key tied to my account? I'd hate for the ladies to miss the class because a message got stuck in the ether.

Warmly,
Joanne Lawton

Hi Joanne,

Thanks for the detailed follow-up, that's exactly the kind of information that helps me track this down. I pulled up your account and I can see the API key is still active, but there was a brief delivery queue backlog on our end Tuesday afternoon that affected a batch of broadcast-list messages, which lines up with what you're describing. I've requeued the stuck ones and they should have gone through by now, but let me know if Dot still hasn't seen hers.

One thing worth checking: are the messages that failed all going to contacts saved under a broadcast list rather than an individual chat? That distinction matters for how the API routes them, and it would help me confirm whether this is fully resolved or just papered over for now.

Also, no rush at all, but if you get a chance, screenshot the little status icons next to the messages that stalled. That'll tell me if it's a delivery issue or a read-receipt issue, which are two different animals.

Best,
David

Hello David,

I checked this morning after my walk with Dot, and wouldn't you know it, she'd finally gotten the message, so whatever you did on your end seems to have shaken it loose. You're right that the stuck ones were all going out through that broadcast list rather than to folks one by one, so that's a useful clue for you to keep in your back pocket.

I'll send along a screenshot next time it happens rather than making you guess, that's fair. For now I think we can call this one settled, and I'm grateful you took the time to walk me through it instead of just telling me to reinstall something, which is what the last fellow tried.

Give Karen and the kids my love, and tell Emily I want to hear all about her clinicals when she has a spare minute. I'll be here with my tea and my programs by nine thirty as usual.

Fondly,
Joanne

Dear David,

I hope this finds you well. I'm writing about that walking app you all set me up with a few weeks back — the one that's meant to log my morning route with Dot Henderson. Ever since Tuesday it's been telling me we walked clear to Gresham and back, which would be a miracle at our age, bad hips and all, but I suspect it's just gotten confused.

Dot and I go out every morning rain or shine, down past the Fremont Bridge and back before I need to get Karen's grocery list texted off and settle in for my programs. It's not a big matter, but I like to keep an honest record, the Lord knows I've kept enough charts in my day to know when a number's lying to me.

Could someone look into it? No rush, just when you get a chance.

Warmly,
Joanne Lawton

Dear Mrs. Lawton,

Thank you for writing in, and I'm sorry for the trouble with your route tracking. That does sound like a GPS drift issue — sometimes the signal bounces around near bridges or tall buildings and the app stitches together a route that never happened, Gresham included.

A couple of questions so I can sort this out properly: does this happen every morning, or just on certain days? And do you recall if your phone was in your coat pocket versus your bag during those walks? Sometimes the position makes a difference.

In the meantime, I'd suggest toggling the location setting off and back on before your next walk with Dot — that often resets the drift. I'll also flag your account on our end so our engineers can take a look at the raw data from Tuesday.

Thanks for your patience,
David

Dear David,

Thank you for looking into this so quickly. To answer your questions — it's happened three mornings now, always the ones where it's misty out, which I suppose makes sense if it's a signal problem like you said. And yes, it's been in my coat pocket, the zippered one, since I don't like carrying a bag when I'm walking.

I did as you suggested and toggled the setting before Dot and I set out this morning. We went our usual loop, nothing fancy, just enough to get the blood moving before I come home to check on Brian's old fishing gear he keeps meaning to pick up. The app behaved itself today, showed the right route for once.

I'll keep an eye on it the rest of the week and let you know if the mist brings it back. Emily thinks I fuss over it more than I need to, but I told her a nurse never trusts a chart she hasn't double-checked herself.

Blessings,
Joanne

Dear Mrs. Lawton,

I'm glad to hear this morning's walk logged correctly! It does sound like the location reset resolved the immediate issue, and our engineering team confirmed there was a brief GPS calibration error affecting accounts in the Portland area on foggy mornings — you were not imagining things, and your instinct to double-check was the right one.

We've pushed a small fix that should reduce the drift going forward, even on misty days. If it crops up again this week, just reply here and I'll escalate it further rather than have you keep resetting the toggle yourself.

Please tell Dot Henderson her walking partner runs a tighter ship than most of our engineers. I hope you both enjoy your walk tomorrow, and that Brian finally comes for that fishing gear.

Best,
David

Dear David,

I hope you and Karen are keeping well, and that Tyler's baseball is going better than that rained-out game two weeks back. I'm writing because that little reminder chime on the tablet for my morning check-in has gone quiet the past three mornings. I do my walk with Dot Henderson first thing, come home, do what I need to do at the kitchen table, and normally the thing chimes right on cue after. Now it's silent as a church mouse.

I don't want to make a fuss, and I know you didn't build the confounded thing, but you're the one who set it up on my tablet at Thanksgiving, so you're my first call. Could it just need a nudge, or did one of those updates knock it sideways? I'd rather not miss logging things in the mornings, habit is half of health as far as I'm concerned, that and a good cup of nettle tea.

No rush, dear, I know you've got the clinic hours too. Just let me know what to poke at.

Love,
Joanne

Hi Joanne,

Thanks for flagging this, and don't worry, you're not making a fuss at all. This is exactly the kind of thing I'd rather you call about than just let slide. From what you're describing it sounds like the reminder notification got muted during the last software update, which happens more than it should. It's not that anything is broken with your account or your history, just a setting that reset itself.

Could you go to Settings, then Notifications, and check that the morning reminder toggle is switched on? If it's already on, toggling it off and back on usually forces it to re-register. If that doesn't do the trick, I can remote in this weekend when I'm over for dinner and sort it properly, no charge to you obviously.

Glad the walks with Dot are still happening rain or shine. Give her our best, and tell Emily good luck balancing clinicals with everything else this month.

Take care,
David

Hello David,

I hope this finds you well. I'm writing because Karen mentioned I should let you know how things went after we set up that little reminder chime on my tablet for my PBS shows. I do love my dramas — Sunday night is Masterpiece, and I won't have anyone ringing me during Call the Midwife, not even Karen with her grocery questions. The chime works lovely, thank you.

My only trouble is the tablet seems to want to stay lit up and chirping well past 9:30, and an old nurse like me knows a body needs her sleep on schedule, same as she'd tell any patient. Is there a way to have it hush itself after a certain hour so I'm not tempted to keep watching past my bedtime? I've got my morning walk with Dot Henderson bright and early and I like to be sharp for it.

Thank you for your patience with an old woman and her gadgets.

Warmly,
Joanne Lawton

Dear Mrs. Lawton,

Thank you for writing in, and I'm glad to hear the reminder chime has been working well for your PBS lineup — Call the Midwife is a fine choice to protect from interruptions! I completely understand wanting the tablet to settle down after a certain hour.

What you're looking for is called a "Do Not Disturb" or "Wind Down" schedule. If you go to Settings, then look for either "Do Not Disturb" or "Digital Wellbeing," you can set it to automatically dim the screen and silence notifications starting at 9:30 PM each night. It will stay that way until morning, so you won't be tempted to keep scrolling once your show ends.

Would you like me to walk you through those exact steps over the phone, or would a simple written checklist work better for you? I want to make sure it's easy to set once and then just leave alone.

Best regards,
David Mitchell
Support Team

Dear David,

A written checklist would suit me just fine — I can tape it inside my kitchen cabinet where I keep my reading glasses, and Emily can double-check my work when she's over between her nursing clinicals. Between you and me, she rolls her eyes at how attached I am to that tablet, but she also knows better than to argue with a woman who raised two children and worked the night shift for thirty years.

I tried poking around in the Settings myself before your reply came through and found something called Wind Down, so perhaps I'm not as hopeless as I look. If your checklist confirms I did it right, I'll consider the matter closed and get back to my shows in peace.

Thank you kindly for your help, David. You've got a good manner about you — must be why Karen married you.

Blessings,
Joanne Lawton

Subject: Grocery list app - still not texting Karen right

Hello David,

Hope you're doing well. I'm writing about that little grocery list feature we set up last week - the one where I'm supposed to be able to send my list straight to Karen's phone without fussing with it myself. Twice now I typed up my list Tuesday evening, same as I always do before Dot and I take our walk the next morning, and it never went through to her. She said she never got a peep.

I'm not one to complain over nothing, but I did want it looked at since it's become part of my little routine. If you could check on it I'd be much obliged.

Warm regards,
Joanne Lawton

Subject: Re: Grocery list app - still not texting Karen right

Dear Mrs. Lawton,

Thank you for letting me know, and I'm sorry for the trouble. I looked into your account and it does look like the message is getting stuck before it sends rather than going out and getting lost somewhere in between, which is good news in a way - it means we can fix it on our end.

Could you tell me roughly what time Tuesday evening you tried, and whether you saved the list as a draft first or typed it fresh each time? That will help me narrow down what's happening.

Best,
David Mitchell
Support Team

Subject: Re: Grocery list app - still not texting Karen right

Hello David,

I typed it fresh both times, around 7 o'clock or so, right after my shows wound down for the night. I don't fuss with drafts - never have, not since my days on the ward when you learned to write things down once and get on with it. Both times I hit send and it looked to go through on my end, little checkmark and all, but Karen swears up and down her phone never buzzed.

I did notice her number in there still has the old area code from before she and David moved, if that matters any. I meant to update it and never got round to it.

Take care,
Joanne

Subject: Re: Grocery list app - still not texting Karen right

Dear Mrs. Lawton,

That was it exactly - the outdated area code was causing the message to fail silently on our end, which is why it looked sent to you but never arrived for Karen. I've corrected her number in your contacts and sent a test message through myself, which she confirmed she received a few minutes ago.

Your list should go through just fine from here on, right on schedule with your evening routine. Please do write back if anything seems off again, and thank you for your patience while we sorted it out.

Warmly,
David Mitchell
Support Team
